title: Som Tam

overview.description: A Thai green papaya salad combining crunchy shredded papaya with lime, fish sauce, palm sugar, garlic, chilli, tomatoes and peanuts.

ingredients: 1 medium green papaya, peeled and deseeded
1 small handful Thai basil or fresh coriander, optional
method: Peel the green papaya.
Cut it in half and remove the seeds.
Shred the flesh into thin strips.
If you do not have a papaya shredder, use a julienne peeler or sharp knife.
Place the garlic and chillies in a large mortar.
Pound gently until roughly crushed.
Add the peanuts and pound lightly so some are crushed while others remain in pieces.
Add the palm sugar, lime juice and fish sauce.
Stir until the sugar is mostly dissolved.
Add the green beans.
Pound gently while turning the mixture to bruise the beans without completely crushing them.
Add the tomatoes.
Pound lightly to release some of their juices.
Add the shredded green papaya.
Use the pestle and a spoon together to gently pound and toss the salad.
Continue until the papaya has absorbed the dressing.
Add the dried shrimp if using.
Taste and adjust the balance of lime, fish sauce, sugar and chilli.
Finish with the remaining peanuts and herbs if using.
Serve immediately.

learning.did-you-know: Som tam means 'pounded sour' in Thai, referring to the preparation method and the characteristic sharp flavour of the salad.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How pounding and bruising ingredients releases flavour and changes texture, and how to balance the five major flavour sensations of Thai cooking.
skills: Balancing sweet, sour, salty and spicy flavours

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A vibrant and highly distinctive complete meal salad that introduces a completely different flavour balance and preparation technique from the Mediterranean, Middle Eastern and South Asian salads already in the collection.
chapman-tips: The green papaya should be firm and crisp, not ripe and sweet.
A julienne peeler works well if you don't have a traditional papaya shredder.
Don't simply stir the salad; gently pounding it is part of what gives Som Tam its character.
The ideal flavour balance is personal, so taste and adjust as you go.
Start with little or no chilli for the children and add extra chilli to individual portions.
Fish sauce provides saltiness and umami, not just a fish flavour.
Don't crush all the peanuts into powder; some texture is desirable.
If green papaya is unavailable, firm green mango makes an excellent alternative.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The papaya gradually loses its crisp texture once dressed.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten immediately. Undressed shredded papaya can be stored separately and dressed just before eating.
